coredata.dc:title: Improving broadband absorption of carbon dot conjugated melanin via pre-doping strategy as interfacial photothermal evaporator
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kan, Guoyu
Li, Yukong
Chang, Qing
Deng, Xiaoyong
Xie, Yijun
coredata.dc:description: Photothermal seawater desalination harnesses solar energy to induce heat and expediates seawater evaporation and purification. The desalination efficacy heavily relies on the light absorption capability and microstructure of the solar evaporator. Polydopamine (PDA) represents a typical synthetic melanin with broad spectral absorption characteristics. However, its absorption capacity within the near-infrared (NIR) region is limited, leading to inadequate photothermal conversion efficiency. To overcome this challenge, we adopt a straightforward approach involving the pre-doping of N,S-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) with dopamine through covalent interaction to fabricate CQDs-loaded mesoporous polydopamine (MPDA-8). This strategy effectively reduces the band gap of PDA by fostering additional donor–acceptor pairs and π-stacking structures, thereby broadening the absorption capacity across the UV–Vis-NIR spectrum through nonradiative transitions. The MPDA-8 coated Balsa wood and cellulose evaporator achieved high evaporation rates of 1.82 kg m−2 h−1 and 2.10 kg m−2 h−1, respectively, overcoming the limit of 2D interfacial evaporator. The small pores and fiber channels within the wood, coupled with the hydrophilicity of MPDA-8, facilitates efficient water transportation and reduces evaporation enthalpy. Outdoor evaporation experiments conducted under sunlight corroborated the practical viability of this material. This study introduces a new perspective for advancing synthetic melanins in photothermal applications through the nanoparticle pre-doping strategy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solar-driven seawater desalination
Carbon quantum dots
Polydopamine
Pre-doping
Photothermal conversion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of synthetic melanin-based wood composite
Steam generation and solar desalination experiments
Structural analysis and photothermal mechanism of MPDA-i

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22205134, 82104220) and State Key Laboratory of Polymeric Material Design and Synthesis for Biomedical Function, Soochow University (SDHY2203). We also acknowledge the contributions of Zhen Zhang and Prof. Peng Zhang from Shanghai University, and the Materials Characterization Center at the East China Normal University Multifunctional Platform for Innovation, for providing the scanning electron microscopy and transient absorption characterization, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of MPDA-coated wood for the application of solar desalination.

(a) The synthetic procedure of MPDA-i through the co-polymerization of N,S-CQDs and MPDA. (b) FESEM image of MPDA-8 with TEM as an inset (scale bar = 100 nm). (c) FTIR spectra of MPDA-i and N,S-CQDs. (d) UV–Vis spectra of MPDA-i, N,S-CQDs, and PDA. (e) TEM-EDS mapping of MPDA-8 (scale bar = 100 nm). (f) DLS of MPDA-i. (g) XPS survey spectra of MPDA-i (i = 0,4,8,12,16); the inset is S 2p XPS. (h) C 1s, (i) O 1s, and (j) Mn 2p peaks in XPS spectra of MPDA-8.

(a) L* values of different MPDA solutions at 100 µg mL−1. (b) Photothermal imaging of MPDA-0, MPDA-4, MPDA-8, PDA, and water under 808 nm laser at 1.5 W cm−2. (c) The photothermal response of MPDA-i (i = 0, 4, 8, 12), PDA, mPDA and N,S-CQDs aqueous solutions (200µg mL−1) over a period of 600 s under 808 nm laser irradiation. (d) Temperature elevations of MPDA-8 at different concentrations under 808 nm laser irradiation. (e) UV absorbance at 808 nm and its photothermal temperature for different MPDA-i and mPDA. (f) Temperature curves of MPDA-8 under five on/off cycles of laser irradiation. (g) Photothermal parameters of different PDA samples and CQDs.

(a) Schematic illustration of the growth of MPDA-8 nanoparticle. (b) The TEM images of MPDA-8 nanoparticle growing at different reaction time. (c) fs-TA spectra of MPDA-8 and PDA at 2 ps after excitation. (d) fs-TA spectra of MPDA-8 at specified delay times. (e) 2.5-D color diagram depicting the transient absorption spectra of PDA. (f) ESA kinetic traces of MPDA-8 at detection wavelength of 914 nm. The black solid line shows the power law fitting of the MPDA-8. (g) fs-TA spectra of PDA at specified delay times. (h) 2.5-D color diagram depicting the transient absorption spectra of MPDA-8.

(a) ESP distributions of DHI, IQ, CQD and their histograms of ESP area distribution. (b) Energy level diagrams of MPDA-i, PDA, mPDA, N, S-CQDs. (c) Typical chemical structure in MPDA-i with theoretically calculated energy band gaps and π-π stacking using DFT simulations at the B3LYP/6-31 G* level. H1: DHI-DHI, H2: IQ-IQ, H3: CQDs, H4: DHI-CQDs, and H5: IQ-CQDs.

(a) The schematic diagram of indoor solar desalination experimental. (b) SEM cross-section image of MPDA-8-coated wood structure (scale bar = 1 μm). (c) UV–Vis-NIR diffuse reflection spectra of wood, PDA-coated wood and MPDA-8-coated wood in the wavelength of 250 to 2500 nm. (d) Thermal infrared images of different samples under one sun illumination for 300 s. (e) DSC measurement of pure water and water on the surface of MPDA-8-coated wood. (f) Evaporation rates of saline, wood, PDA and MPDA-8-coated wood under one sun illumination. (g) The ion concentrations of the saline and seawater collected from Bohai Bay before and after desalination. The dashed lines refer to the standard for drinkable water from the WHO and EPA. (h) Plot on the evaporation efficiency and evaporation rate of different samples. (i) Evaporation rate of MPDA-8 coated wood used for 40 h of desalination experiments.

(a) Photographs of outdoor solar evaporation experiment at 15:47 11/2/2023. (b) Sunlight intensity and evaporation rate during the outdoor test. (c) Outdoor temperature, surface temperature of MPDA-8 coated wood and humidity during the outdoor test. (d) Water contact angle on the surface of MPDA-8 coated wood. (e) UV–Vis spectra of RhB and MB (50 µg mL−1) before and after evaporation. (f) Photographs of the wood treated with salt crystals on its surface under one sun irradiation over time. (g) The change of four metal ion concentrations in pond before and after desalination. (h) Comparison of evaporation rates and efficiencies of different photothermal materials under one sun irradiation. (i) Comparison of the evaporation rates (m⁎), evaporation efficiencies (η), contact angle, water evaporation enthalpy (Hv), and surface evaporation temperature (T) in different PDA composites.

head.ce:title: Improving broadband absorption of carbon dot conjugated melanin via pre-doping strategy as interfacial photothermal evaporator

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Science and Engineering, Shanghai Key Laboratory of Chemical Assessment and Sustainability, Tongji University, Shanghai 200092, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Science and Engineering
Shanghai Key Laboratory of Chemical Assessment and Sustainability

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Science and Engineering, Shanghai Key Laboratory of Chemical Assessment and Sustainability, Tongji University, Shanghai 200092, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photothermal seawater desalination harnesses solar energy to induce heat and expediates seawater evaporation and purification. The desalination efficacy heavily relies on the light absorption capability and microstructure of the solar evaporator. Polydopamine (PDA) represents a typical synthetic melanin with broad spectral absorption characteristics. However, its absorption capacity within the near-infrared (NIR) region is limited, leading to inadequate photothermal conversion efficiency. To overcome this challenge, we adopt a straightforward approach involving the pre-doping of N,S-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) with dopamine through covalent interaction to fabricate CQDs-loaded mesoporous polydopamine (MPDA-8). This strategy effectively reduces the band gap of PDA by fostering additional donor–acceptor pairs and π-stacking structures, thereby broadening the absorption capacity across the UV–Vis-NIR spectrum through nonradiative transitions. The MPDA-8 coated Balsa wood and cellulose evaporator achieved high evaporation rates of 1.82 kg m−2 h−1 and 2.10 kg m−2 h−1, respectively, overcoming the limit of 2D interfacial evaporator. The small pores and fiber channels within the wood, coupled with the hydrophilicity of MPDA-8, facilitates efficient water transportation and reduces evaporation enthalpy. Outdoor evaporation experiments conducted under sunlight corroborated the practical viability of this material. This study introduces a new perspective for advancing synthetic melanins in photothermal applications through the nanoparticle pre-doping strategy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing wastage of water resources, freshwater on earth is becoming increasingly scarce. Simultaneously, advancements in technology have led to intensified discharge of industrial wastewater, resulting in heavy metal ions contaminating our limited freshwater resources [1]. Desalination of seawater has emerged as a promising solution to address the ever-growing demand for freshwater. Two common desalination strategies, thermal evaporation and reverse osmosis, have been employed for conventional desalination [2]. However, they generally suffered from high energy consumption and operational costs. Solar-driven seawater evaporation is considered a promising green solution to freshwater scarcity [3]. In this process, seawater is transmitted to the hydrophilic evaporator surface, where it undergoes evaporation to yield clean steam owing to photothermal conversion facilitated by the photothermal evaporator, achieving a high seawater evaporation rate [4–6]. Despite progress in this field, challenges persist in addressing issues such as the low photothermal conversion efficiency of the evaporator, as well as inadequate stability and hydrophilicity [7,8].

As a natural melanin mimic, polydopamine (PDA) has attracted considerable interest as a prominent photothermal material due to its intrinsic hydrophilicity and adhesive properties derived from its amino and catechol groups. Furthermore, its cost-effectiveness and ease of synthesis make it highly suitable for practical applications [9–13]. The presence of highly delocalized π-electron structures, facilitated by donor–acceptor pairs within PDA, enables broad-spectrum light absorption and efficient conversion of light into heat through nonradiative relaxation processes, which endows PDA with promising applications in seawater desalination [14,15]. However, it is noteworthy that PDA exhibits limited absorption in the near-infrared (NIR) region. Consequently, substantial enhancements in photothermal performance can be realized by modulating its molecular structure and functionalizing it to boost NIR absorption [3,16]. Yang et al. introduced benzimidazole doping to modify the structure of PDA and amplified light absorption capacity. This molecular strategy offers a viable approach for designing and fabricating PDA-based materials with superior photothermal properties [17].

The conventional approach to structural modification involves the use of small molecules to enhance the photothermal performance of PDA. For instance, Zou et al. employed arginine doping to modify PDA, which was subsequently coated onto wood substrates for efficient solar evaporation [18]. However, small molecule conjugation possesses complex synthetic steps and inadequate stability. On the other hand, NIR-responsive carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit a large surface area, high NIR light absorption, and tunable optical properties [19,20]. Rahmawati et al. developed nitrogen-doped CQDs for photothermal seawater desalination applications [21]. Compared with small molecule-doped polydopamine, PDA functionalized with CQDs offers broad-spectrum absorption with easy preparation, particularly in the NIR region. Our previous study has indicated that surface modification of PDA by CQDs can significantly enhance their photothermal efficiency in cancer therapy [13]. However, a drawback of surface modification is the relatively low stability and loading amount of CQDs on the PDA surface, which may result in their release during applications. If CQDs are conjugated with PDA during dopamine polymerization (pre-doping), the final composites may simultaneously increase stability and exhibit high absorption capacity. Therefore, we propose a pre-doping method to integrate CQDs into the internal structure of PDA, aiming to enhance photothermal efficiency applicable in seawater desalination.

In this study, we pre-doped nitrogen/sulfur-doped CQDs (N,S-CQDs) into Mn3+-loaded mesoporous polydopamine (MPDA-i, where i denotes the mass of CQDs) for photothermal desalination. CQDs were covalently bonded to PDA through the introduction of CSC bonds via Michael addition, which reduced the bandgap of PDA by increasing the π-conjugated structure and donor–acceptor pairs and showed minimal radiative transitions (Fig. 1 ). This was confirmed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis, density functional theory (DFT) simulations, and femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy, further enhancing the light absorption capacity of PDA. Additionally, MPDA-i was coated on an environmentally friendly and cost-effective wood substrate with self-floating capability for simulated seawater desalination experiments. The MPDA-i coating reduced evaporation enthalpy during the photothermal conversion process, while also enhancing the hydrophilicity and salt resistance. Under an irradiance of 1 kW m−2, we attained an evaporation rate of 1.82 kg m−2 h−1 with a high desalination efficiency of 95.6 %. Furthermore, the material exhibited outstanding performance in outdoor sunlight desalination, achieving a rate of 1.1 kg m−2 h−1 under maximum irradiance of 0.9 kW m−2. Our study demonstrates that MPDA-8 exhibits a superior evaporation rate compared to other 2D evaporators coated with polymers, metals, and carbon-based materials. In comparison to other small molecule-conjugated PDA composites, the MPDA-8 evaporator we developed shows significant enhancements in seawater evaporation rate, efficiency, contact angle, surface temperature, and reduced evaporation enthalpy. Moreover, employing natural wood as the substrate offers several advantages, including lower cost, environmental sustainability, and greater availability compared to alternative substrates. It also displayed efficient filtration capabilities for metal ions and dye pollutants, suggesting promising applications in solar-powered seawater desalination.

Citric acid (2.0 g) and urea (6.0 g) were dissolved in 30 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). The solution was then transferred to a hydrothermal autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. After the solution was cooled, the products were obtained by centrifugation at 20,000 rpm for 20 min, and the nanoparticles were precipitated by adding ethanol. Finally, the final products were freeze-dried to solid powders.

65 mL of deionized (DI) water and 60 mL of anhydrous ethanol were mixed together. Next, 60 mg of Mn(CH3COO)3·2H2O, 360 mg of Pluronic F-127, and different amounts (4, 8, 12, 16 mg) of N,S-CQDs were added to the mixture. The solution was stirred at 1500 rpm for 10 min before adding 740 μL of 1,3,5-trimethylbenzene. After stirring for another 10 min, 180 mg of Tris dissolved in 10 mL of water was added to the mixture. Subsequently, after 5 min, 120 mg of dopamine hydrochloride was dissolved in 10 mL of DI water was added to the mixture for overnight stirring. The final product was collected by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm with DI water multiple times. After that, the solution was sonicated in a mixed solvent (ethanol: acetone = 2:1) for three times for 30 min each time to remove TMB. Finally, the nanoparticle was collected by centrifugation to obtain MPDA-i.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of synthetic melanin-based wood composite
ce:section.ce:para: 10 mg of MPDA-8 or PDA nanoparticles were dispersed in 0.5 mL of deionized water. The resulting solution was dropwise coated a wood substrate (Diameter = 4 cm; Thickness = 1 cm). Subsequently, the PDA-wood composites were placed in an oven at 70 °C for 10 min. After cooling, the composites were stored in a petri dish for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steam generation and solar desalination experiments
ce:section.ce:para: The device was irradiated by a solar simulator (CEL-PE300L-3A), and the evaporation rate was measured for 60 min at a steady-state condition. In a 100 mL beaker, the wood coated with MPDA-8 was placed on the surface of saline and then weighed on a scale to observe any mass changes.

The MPDA-8 loaded wood evaporation device was placed in a beaker containing pond water. The purification capacity and evaporation efficiency of the MPDA-8 coated wood device for outdoor pond water were determined by analyzing the obtained water. The light intensity of the outdoor sun was measured using a TS5-LP100 optical power meter.

Herein, we employed a pre-doping method to co-polymerize CQDs with dopamine and Mn3+ ions in the presence of 1,3,5-trimethylbenzene and F127, resulting in the formation of mesoporous MPDA-i (i = 0, 4, 8, 12, 16, Fig. 2
a). Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and dynamic light scattering (DLS) revealed the uniform size distribution and porous morphology of MPDA-i (i = 4, 8, 12, 16) with negative zeta potentials (Figs. 2b, f, S1 and S2). Specifically, the particle size showed a slight increase from 146 ± 20 nm to 175 ± 29 nm with the increasing loading of CQDs. We also synthesized metal-free mesoporous PDA (mPDA) according to literature [22], and their morphology remained unchanged with or without Mn3+ loading. The diameter of N,S-CQDs was determined to be 2–5 nm (Fig. S3). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller measurements indicated the surface area of MPDA-0 was 36.9 m2 g−1, while that of MPDA-8 was 25.3 m2 g−1, demonstrating successful loading of N,S-CQDs in MPDA-8 (Fig. S4). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra revealed distinct peaks at 1680 cm−1 corresponding to the CO bond and at 1080 cm−1 attributed to the CS and CN bonds, similar to those observed in N,S-CQDs (Fig. 2c). UV–Vis spectra indicated the Mn3+ loading (MPDA-0) enhanced UV–Vis absorption compared to PDA due to d-d transitions (Fig. 2d) [23,24]. The doping level of Mn was determined to be 7.7 wt% using inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES). Following pre-doping with different amounts of CQDs, MPDA-i exhibited significant absorption enhancement from 600 – 1000 nm (Fig. 2d). The absorption peak in CQDs observed at 600 nm in the NIR region is likely attributed to the n → π* transition, which arises from the extensive conjugated system and the high degree of electron delocalization within the carbon core [25,26]. Intriguingly, the absorption of MPDA-8 was the highest, and it decreased after pre-doping more than 8 mg of CQDs, possibly due to partial structural collapse in MPDA-12 and MPDA-16 resulting from excessively high CQDs loadings (Fig. S1). To further support the advantage of pre-doping over post-modification strategies, 8 mg of CQDs was physically mixed with MPDA-0, resulting in only slight enhancement of UV absorption in the NIR region, indicating lower CQDs encapsulation compared to MPDA-8 using the pre-doping strategy (Fig. S5).

According to TEM energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping (Fig. 2e), it was observed that various elements (C, N, O, Mn, S) were uniformly distributed on the nanoparticles. The loading ratio of CQDs was 3.6 wt% for MPDA-8 from thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. S6). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) also confirmed the presence of C, N, O, Mn, and S in the spectra of all MPDA samples (Fig. 2g). C 1s was composed of four types of carbon bonds: sp2-C (CC) peak at 284.3 eV, sp3-C (CC) peak at 284.8 eV, CO/N/S at 286.1 eV, and CO/N at 288.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 2h) [3]. And OC at 532.6 eV, ON/S at 531.4 eV and OC at 530.4 eV were observed in the O 1s peak (Figs. 2I and S7) [27]. For Mn 2p XPS spectra, the characteristic peaks of 641.5 eV and 644.8 eV were attributed to Mn 2p3/2 and peaks of 653.2 eV and 656.4 eV were attributed to Mn 2p1/2 [28–31]. In the S 2p region, two peaks were attributed to the CSO3 H bond at 168.8 eV and the CSC bond at 163.6 eV, whereas the S 2p in N,S-CQDs exhibited peaks corresponding to CSO3 H (169.2 eV) and CSO2 C bonds (166.2 eV). The appearance of new CSC bond suggests the existence of covalent interaction between CQDs and PDA through Michael addition (Figs. 2g and S8d) [19]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns exhibited a prominent graphite peak at 26.4° for N,S-CQDs, indicating a high degree of graphitization, along with an amorphous peak characteristic of PDA (Fig. S9) [32]. Raman spectra revealed that the intensity of the ordered G-band at 1590 cm−1 was stronger than that of the disordered D-band at 1390 cm−1, which was attributed to the typical G (graphitic carbon) and D (disordered carbon) bands of carbon materials [33]. These bands were also related to the stretching and deformation vibrations of the catechol moiety in PDA molecules (Fig. S10).

The light absorption and photothermal response of MPDA-i were investigated. MPDA-8 exhibited the darkest color and the lowest lightness values (L*, a*, b*) compared to other MPDA-i nanoparticles, which corresponded precisely to the UV–Vis results (Figs. 3 a and S11). We also compared the photothermal performance of MPDA-i, PDA, and mPDA under 20 min of light on/off cycles, and all MPDA-i samples exhibited a higher temperature increase compared to MPDA-0 due to the pre-doping of CQDs, with MPDA-8 reaching the highest temperature of 53 ℃ under 1.5 W/cm2 of 808 nm irradiation (Fig. 3b–e). Compared to PDA, mPDA demonstrated an enhanced photothermal conversion capability owing to its abundant pore structures, enabling efficient heat dissipation. The temperature increases of MPDA-8 displayed a concentration-/power density-dependent behavior, reaching a temperature of 58 ℃ at 2 W/cm2. MPDA-8, MPDA-0 and PDA showed good photothermal stability under five laser on/off cycles (Figs. 3f and S12). The photothermal conversion efficiency of MPDA-8 was calculated as 47.6 %, surpassing that of MPDA-16 (44.7 %), MPDA-0 (30.7 %), and CQDs (41.4 %), respectively, comparable with many distinguished photothermal agents, including polyaniline, carbon materials, metal composites, gold nanoparticles, and MXene (Figs. 3g and S13) [3,25,34–40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analysis and photothermal mechanism of MPDA-i
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore how CQDs influence the growth mechanism of MPDA during the polymerization process via pre-doping methodology, we studied the time-dependent growth of MPDA-0 and MPDA-8. MPDA-0 formed discernible nanoparticles rapidly within 2 min and stabilized in 10 min. On the contrary, MPDA-8 exhibited a distinctly different dendritic structure within 2 min. Then nanoparticles adhered to the dendritic structure and gradually formed stable nanoparticles after 4 min (Fig. 4
a, b, S14 , and S15). There was a noticeable increase in nanoparticle size for MPDA-8 compared to MPDA-0 (Fig. S16a and b). The pre-doping of CQDs enables alteration of the internal structure of MPDA, thereby significantly influencing the polymerization process of MPDA and its photothermal property. Femtosecond transient absorption (fs-TA) spectroscopy was employed to investigate the photon excitation behavior within the molecular structure [41,42]. Upon pump pulse excitation (400 nm), nanoparticles exhibit transient absorption bands in the NIR region (850 ∼ 1400 nm) due to the phenomenon of excited-state absorption (ESA) (Figs. S17 and S18) [43]. By comparing MPDA-8 and PDA at identical delay time, it was observed that they both exhibited similar spectral profiles, albeit with MPDA-8 displaying a higher absorption intensity, indicating a more rapid response to photon excitation (Fig. 4c). The similar ESA kinetic traces for MPDA-0 and MPDA-8 suggested the excitons exist within the PDA domain and not influenced by the type of doping. Furthermore, a reduction in the intensity of ESA was observed with increasing delay, indicating a rapid recombination rate for excited-state photons (Fig. 4d, e, g, and h). The ESA kinetics curves for MPDA-8 and PDA were analyzed using a power law model, yielding an exponent of −0.392 ± 0.008 and −0.387 ± 0.007, respectively (Table S2). This finding suggests a broad distribution of exciton recombination and high mobility and diffusivity of excitons within the internal structure of PDA (Fig. 4f). It is known that CQDs exhibit strong fluorescence signals, whereas MPDA-i does not exhibit any detectable fluorescence or phosphorescence signals, indicating the absence of radiative relaxation as fluorescence and phosphorescence for MPDA-i (Fig. S19). Therefore, although the incorporation of CQDs enhances light absorption, the rate of exciton recombination remains comparable to that of pure PDA, approximately around 1 ns (Fig. S20b), smaller than the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes. This implies an efficient and rapid nonradiative relaxation process to thermal energy from the excited state under light stimulation (Fig. S20).

To study the impact of covalently doped CQDs on the energy structure of PDA, we conducted electrochemical CV measurements to acquire band gap information (Figs. 5
b, S21a and b). The CV tests enable the calculation of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels of these samples according to the equation:
E
HOMO / L U M O
= - e
(
E
ox
/
E
red
+ 4.44 )
e V
, calculated in terms of oxidation onset potentials (
E
ox
) and reduction onset potentials (
E
red
). The
E
HOMO / L U M O
values were determined as follows: −4.73/−3.8 eV for PDA, −4.75/−3.85 eV for MPDA-0, −4.7/−4.04 eV for MPDA-8, −4.76/−3.94 eV for MPDA-16, and −4.64/−4.1 eV for CQDs, respectively. The energy band gap (
E g
) of MPDA-8 (0.66 eV) is smaller than that of PDA (0.93 eV) and other MPDA-i samples with varying CQDs doping, consistent with the UV–Vis data. We attribute the reduction in band gap after pre-doping with CQDs to the formation of donor–acceptor pairs and π-conjugated structures between 5,6-dihydroxyindole (DHI)/indole-5,6-quinone (IQ) and CQDs. To validate this hypothesis, we conducted DFT calculations to determine the energy band gap of representative structures within MPDA-i at the molecular level. The electrostatic potentials (ESP) of DHI, IQ, and CQDs were first analyzed, and the results indicated that negative ESP values were distributed throughout the DHI molecules, while positive ESP values dominated the IQ and CQD molecular structures, indicating their strong affinity for electrons relative to DHI (Fig. 5a). The calculated Eg values of DHI-CQDs (3.80 eV) and IQ-CQDs (3.28 eV) were lower than those of DHI-DHI (6.51 eV) and IQ-IQ (3.94 eV), respectively. These findings suggest the formation of donor–acceptor pairs between DHI and the electrophilic IQ and CQDs through CSC bonds (Figs. 5c and S22). Various overlapping structures of DHI-DHI and IQ-IQ were examined, revealing a consistent trend in energy gap across different morphologies (Fig. S23). Furthermore, the π-π stacking interaction of PDA exhibited a notable increase from −86.7 to −129.9 kJ/mol upon conjugation with CQDs. This tight arrangement of π-conjugated bonds can contribute to enhanced photothermal conversion [44]. Overall, the covalent interaction between CQDs and PDA facilitates the formation of new DHI-CQD pairs alongside existing DHI-IQ pairs. They induce rehybridization of molecular orbitals, consequently reducing the energy gaps between HOMO and LUMO, thereby contributing to good photothermal performance [36,45].

To investigate the photothermal evaporation performance of MPDA-8 in solar-driven seawater desalination, MPDA-8 was coated on Balsa wood to serve as the evaporator (Fig. 6 a). Balsa wood possesses remarkable water absorption capabilities owing to the abundance of hydroxyl groups in its cellulose and hemicellulose microstructure [18,46–48]. Furthermore, the microstructure of wood comprises numerous small pores and fiber channels, enabling efficient water transport (Fig. S29e). SEM confirmed the uniform deposition of MPDA-8 nanoparticles on the wood substrate (Fig. 6b). XPS analysis revealed a uniform loading of MPDA-8 on the wood surface, with no significant spectral changes observed after exposure to sunlight for three days. This indicates the stability of the MPDA-8 coating (Fig. S24). UV–Vis-NIR diffuse reflection characterization revealed a stronger light absorption capacity of MPDA-8 compared to PDA, MPDA-0, MPDA-4, MPDA-12 across the solar spectrum (Figs. 6c and S25). During exposure to one sun illumination, the wet surface temperature of the MPDA-8-based evaporator reached 41.2 °C, as recorded by infrared thermal imaging during 300 s of irradiation, whereas the temperature of PDA only reached 34.1 °C (Figs. 6d and S26). The differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis revealed that the vaporization enthalpy of bulk water is 2257 J g−1, while that of water in the MPDA-8 coated wood was 1998 J g−1, very similar to the calculated value (1933 J g−1) (Fig. 6e, Table S3). The porous structure and large surface area of MPDA-8-coated wood, along with capillary action, contribute to the reduction of water evaporation enthalpy. Fig. 6f demonstrated the evaporation rate for wood, PDA-coated wood and MPDA-8 coated wood was 0.676, 1.125 and 1.824 kg m−2 h−1, respectively (Fig. 6e). It was observed that the solar evaporation efficiency of MPDA-8 coated wood reached 95.6 %, which was significantly higher than that of PDA (59.9 %) and wood (32.3 %) (Fig. 6h and Table S4). The device’s evaporation performance was assessed using Bohai Bay seawater for desalination. Changes in metal ion concentrations (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and K+) after desalination were determined by ICP-OES. The results indicated a significantly lower concentration after desalination using MPDA-8 compared to before desalination, falling below the standard levels by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). Moreover, the data revealed no evidence of Mn3+ leakage from MPDA-8 into the water (Fig. 6g). The durability of the MPDA-8-coated wood was also assessed through a 40-hour cycle (Fig. 6i). The surface of the MPDA-8-coated wood demonstrated a significant improvement in hydrophilicity compared to both PDA-coated and untreated wood, with a contact angle of 8° at 0.18 s, reaching 0° at 0.54 s. In contrast, the contact angles of untreated wood and PDA-coated wood were 87° and 32° at 0.18 s, respectively (Figs. 7 d and S27). MPDA-8 coated wood demonstrated a rapid wetting rate on saline solution (Fig. S28). To evaluate the versatility of the MPDA-8 on other substrates, we tested the evaporation rate of MPDA-8-coated cellulose film (Fig. S29a–d). The results indicated that MPDA-8 exhibited a seawater evaporation rate of 2.10 kg m−2 h−1, surpassing the theoretical limit of 2D evaporator (Fig. S29f).

Fig. 7a depicts the outdoor sunlight evaporation experiment conducted adjacent to the panchi pond of Shanghai University. Vapors emitted from the surface of the MPDA-8 are subsequently condensed into water on the top of the beaker. Throughout November 2, 2023, from 9:00 to 17:00, outdoor temperature, evaporator surface temperature, humidity, light intensity, and hourly evaporation rate were recorded (Fig. 7b and c). The fluctuation in evaporation rate closely followed the variations in outdoor temperature and light intensity, peaking at 1.1 kg m−2 h−1 around 13:00. Outdoor evaporation experiments were conducted over five days in September 2024 (Fig. S30). Daily volumes of evaporated water were collected and compared with the evaporation rates at 13:00 on both sunny and cloudy days. It was observed that on sunny days, with a maximum solar intensity of 1.02 kW m−2 and a maximum ambient temperature of 46.2 °C, the device achieved a peak evaporation rate of 1.31 kg m−2 h−1 and an efficient freshwater production amount of 9.16 kg m−2 over an 8-hour period (Fig. S31), surpassing several previously reported results [7,49]. Additionally, following a 4-day outdoor evaporation experiment utilizing a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, only minimal salt crystallization was observed on the surface of the MPDA-8 evaporator (Fig. S32). To examine the salt dissolution process, an 8-hour outdoor evaporation experiment was conducted using a 10 % NaCl solution. During this experiment, it was observed that the salt on the wood surface migrated from the center toward the edges, with the highest salt accumulation occurring around the evaporator at 13:00, coinciding with peak sunlight intensity. Over time, the salt began to redissolve at the edges due to sufficient water transport from wood substrate (Fig. S33). This reversible crystallization process facilitates the reuse of the evaporator for subsequent cycles. To further evaluate the salt tolerance of the MPDA-8 evaporator, 300 mg of salt crystals were placed on its surface and observed over time under one sun irradiation. The results demonstrated a gradual dissolution of the salts, which ultimately disappeared within 80 min, highlighting the exceptional salt resistance by MPDA-8 (Fig. 7f). The ICP results revealed a marked decrease in metal ion concentrations of pond water after outdoor evaporation, falling below the drinking water standards established by the WHO and EPA. This suggests the MPDA-8 coated wood evaporator is suitable for treating various types of environmental water (Fig. 7g). Additionally, the purification efficacy of the MPDA-8 evaporator was assessed by simulating chemical dye pollutants using methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine B (RhB) solutions. It was observed that their characteristic absorption peaks completely vanished after evaporation, indicating a dye removal rate close to 99.9 % (Fig. 7e). The water transport capacity of the wood substrate significantly influences the solar-driven water evaporation rate. The rapid dye transfer process using rhodamine B along the wood’s transport channels within 30 s demonstrated the excellent water transport properties of the MPDA-8 coated wood (Fig. S34). Overall, our study elucidated that MPDA-8 demonstrated a higher evaporation rate and efficiency compared to other 2D evaporators coated with polymeric, metallic, and carbonaceous materials (Fig. 7h and Table S5) [50–58]. In comparison with three types of PDA materials conjugated with small molecules, the developed MPDA-8 exhibited comprehensive enhancements in both seawater evaporation rate and efficiency (Fig. 7i) [18,35,59].

In summary, this study proposes a straightforward synthetic method for pre-doping N,S-CQDs during the polymerization of mesoporous PDA. The covalent bonding of CQDs and PDA enhances their light absorption capacity and photothermal conversion efficiency without altering the nanoparticle size. The fs-TA spectra and DFT calculations demonstrate that the band gap is reduced due to the formation of new donor–acceptor pairs and π-conjugated structure with minimal radiative relaxation. Furthermore, in desalination experiments using MPDA-8 coated wood, a seawater evaporation rate of approximately 1.82 kg m−2 h−1 and an evaporation efficiency of 95.6 % were achieved, along with 2.10 kg m−2 h−1 using cellulose as the substrate. The heat generated by MPDA-8 during irradiation, combined with the numerous small pores and fiber channels in the microstructure of Balsa wood, facilitates rapid water transmission and reduces the evaporation enthalpy of water. The rapid transfer of dye solution demonstrated the excellent water transport properties of MPDA-8-coated wood. Five days of outdoor evaporation measurements under both sunny and cloudy conditions confirmed the durability and salt resistance of the MPDA-8 coating. Additionally, in outdoor desalination and simulated dye pollutant (RhB and MB) purification experiments, the evaporator exhibited promising performance in practical applications. In conclusion, this study has developed an evaporator that meets the demands of sustainable solar energy-driven seawater desalination and offers a novel design concept for high-evaporation-rate seawater desalination materials.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

Guoyu Kan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yukong Li: Methodology, Data curation. Qing Chang: Resources, Methodology. Xiaoyong Deng: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Yijun Xie: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22205134, 82104220) and State Key Laboratory of Polymeric Material Design and Synthesis for Biomedical Function, Soochow University (SDHY2203). We also acknowledge the contributions of Zhen Zhang and Prof. Peng Zhang from Shanghai University, and the Materials Characterization Center at the East China Normal University Multifunctional Platform for Innovation, for providing the scanning electron microscopy and transient absorption characterization, respectively.
